techniques.evidence_quote: An Uber EXT contractor had their account compromised by an attacker. It is likely that the attacker purchased the contractor's Uber corporate password on the dark web, after the contractor's personal device had been infected with malware, exposing those credentials.
the attacker accessed several other employee accounts which ultimately gave the attacker elevated permissions to a number of tools
the attacker accessed several other employee accounts which ultimately gave the attacker elevated permissions to a number of tools, including G-Suite and Slack
the attacker accessed several other employee accounts which ultimately gave the attacker elevated permissions to a number of tools
the contractor's personal device had been infected with malware, exposing those credentials
Root Cause: Contractor account compromise leading to AWS credential discovery on a shared drive

The attacker then repeatedly tried to log in to the contractor's Uber account. Each time, the contractor received a two-factor login approval request, which initially blocked access. Eventually, however, the contractor accepted one, and the attacker successfully logged in.
the attacker accessed several other employee accounts which ultimately gave the attacker elevated permissions to a number of tools
We also have not found that the attacker accessed any customer or user data stored by our cloud providers (e.g. AWS S3)
it does appear that the attacker downloaded some internal Slack messages, as well as accessed or downloaded information from an internal tool our finance team uses to manage some invoices
We reviewed our codebase and have not found that the attacker made any changes
it does appear that the attacker downloaded some internal Slack messages, as well as accessed or downloaded information from an internal tool our finance team uses to manage some invoices
reconfigured Uber's OpenDNS to display a graphic image to employees on some internal sites

attack_chain.technique_name: Phishing / Malware Infection on Personal Device
attack_chain.description: The contractor's personal device was previously infected with malware, which harvested their Uber corporate credentials. Those credentials were subsequently sold on the dark web.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the contractor's personal device had been infected with malware, exposing those credentials. The attacker then... purchased the contractor's Uber corporate password on the dark web

attack_chain.technique_name: Multi-Factor Authentication Request Generation (MFA Fatigue / Push Bombing)
attack_chain.description: Using the stolen credentials, the attacker repeatedly attempted to log into the contractor's Uber account, triggering multiple MFA push notifications. The contractor eventually approved one, granting the attacker access.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attacker then repeatedly tried to log in to the contractor's Uber account. Each time, the contractor received a two-factor login approval request, which initially blocked access. Eventually, however, the contractor accepted one, and the attacker successfully logged in.

attack_chain.description: After gaining initial access, the attacker discovered AWS credentials stored on a shared internal drive (per incident metadata), enabling lateral movement to cloud resources.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Root Cause: Contractor account compromise leading to AWS credential discovery on a shared drive

attack_chain.technique_name: Valid Accounts (Lateral Movement to Additional Employee Accounts)
attack_chain.description: Using elevated access, the attacker pivoted to several other employee accounts, gaining elevated permissions to internal tools including G-Suite and Slack.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker accessed several other employee accounts which ultimately gave the attacker elevated permissions to a number of tools, including G-Suite and Slack

attack_chain.description: The attacker collected internal Slack messages and financial invoice data from an internal finance tool.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: it does appear that the attacker downloaded some internal Slack messages, as well as accessed or downloaded information from an internal tool our finance team uses to manage some invoices

attack_chain.description: The attacker accessed Uber's AWS S3 environment during the intrusion. Uber confirmed no customer data from cloud providers was accessed, but the attempt was noted as part of the investigation scope.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: We also have not found that the attacker accessed any customer or user data stored by our cloud providers (e.g. AWS S3)

attack_chain.description: The attacker accessed Uber's HackerOne bug bounty dashboard, viewing security researcher-submitted vulnerability reports.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attacker was able to access our dashboard at HackerOne, where security researchers report bugs and vulnerabilities.

attack_chain.description: The attacker reconfigured Uber's OpenDNS to display a graphic image to employees on internal sites, and posted a message to a company-wide Slack channel.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attacker then posted a message to a company-wide Slack channel... and reconfigured Uber's OpenDNS to display a graphic image to employees on some internal sites.

impact_type: Account Takeover / Internal Defacement / Data Collection
initial_access_vector: Stolen contractor credentials purchased on the dark web (harvested via malware infection on personal device), combined with MFA push bombing to bypass two-factor authentication
persistence_mechanism: Lateral movement to additional employee accounts providing sustained elevated access to G-Suite, Slack, and internal tools
privilege_escalation: Access to additional employee accounts with higher permissions after initial contractor account compromise; AWS credentials discovered on a shared internal drive
